county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1868 from Coffee, Dale, and Henry counties. County seat Geneva. Wiregrass region near Florida border. Choctawhatchee River shaped settlement. Peanut, cotton, and timber economy.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Geneva. Created in 1868 from Coffee, Dale, and Henry counties — records are intact from that date. Geneva County is in the Wiregrass region of southeast Alabama, near the Florida line. The Choctawhatchee River shaped settlement and served as a transportation route. The county's economy was based on peanuts, cotton, and timber. The Geneva County Historical Society has local family files. The Wiregrass Museum in Dothan (Houston County) covers the broader region. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds and marriages. For pre-1868 records, check the three parent counties. ADAH holds county tax records and some court records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Geneva County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1819 from Marengo and Tuscaloosa counties. County seat Eutaw. One of the wealthiest antebellum Black Belt counties. Enslaved population exceeded 80% before Civil War. Excellent antebellum architecture in Eutaw.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Eutaw. Greene County records are substantially intact from 1819. The county was one of the wealthiest antebellum Black Belt counties, with massive cotton plantations and enslaved populations exceeding 80% of the total population before the war. Estate inventories and slaveholder tax records at ADAH are critical for African American research. Eutaw's antebellum homes are among the best preserved in Alabama. The Freedmen's Bureau was active in Greene County during Reconstruction. The Greene County Historical Society has local materials. FamilySearch has microfilmed deed books, probate records, and marriages. The county is now one of Alabama's poorest and most rural, with limited local institutional resources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Greene County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1867 from Greene, Marengo, Perry, and Tuscaloosa counties. County seat Greensboro. Black Belt plantation county with high enslaved population. Safe House Black History Museum in Greensboro. Hale County Heritage Book (1994) documents families.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Greensboro. Created in 1867 from Greene, Marengo, Perry, and Tuscaloosa — check parent counties for earlier records. Hale County was another major Black Belt plantation county with high enslaved populations. Greensboro has significant antebellum architecture and planter family records. The Safe House Black History Museum in Greensboro documents African American history. The Hale County Heritage Book (published 1994) contains family histories. Freedmen's Bureau records for Hale County are at NARA and digitized on FamilySearch. The Southern University of Living Faiths was located here. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate, and marriages from 1867.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Hale County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.record_losses: 1853 courthouse fire destroyed most early records; additional losses in Civil War era
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1819 from Conecuh — one of Alabama's original counties. County seat Abbeville. 1853 courthouse fire destroyed most early records; additional Civil War-era losses. Chattahoochee River border with Georgia creates cross-state research needs.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Abbeville. The 1853 courthouse fire destroyed most early records, with additional losses during the Civil War era — this is a burned county. Henry County was one of Alabama's original counties (1819), formed from Conecuh. Check ADAH and FamilySearch for surviving copies. Abbeville was a major town in the Wiregrass region. The county's Chattahoochee River border with Georgia means many families had cross-state connections — check Early County, Georgia records. The Henry County Historical Society has local materials. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records from the post-1853 restart.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1903 from Dale, Geneva, and Henry counties — one of Alabama's newest counties. County seat Dothan ('Peanut Capital of the World'). Wiregrass region commercial center. Fort Rucker influence from neighboring Dale County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Dothan. Created in 1903 from Dale, Geneva, and Henry — this is one of Alabama's newest counties. Dothan is the commercial center of the Wiregrass region and is known as the 'Peanut Capital of the World.' Fort Rucker (now Fort Novosel) in neighboring Dale County has had a major impact on Houston County. The Wiregrass Museum of Art and the Dothan Landmarks Foundation have local history materials. The Houston County Historical Society maintains family files. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate, and marriages from 1903. For earlier records, check Dale, Geneva, and Henry counties. The county's late formation means most records are intact.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1867 fire destroyed courthouse; records partially reconstructed from state copies
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1819 from Cherokee Cession. County seat Scottsboro. 1867 fire destroyed courthouse; records partially reconstructed from state copies. Northeast corner county in Tennessee River valley. Russell Cave National Monument (prehistoric site). Scottsboro Boys case (1931).
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Scottsboro. The 1867 fire destroyed the courthouse and records were partially reconstructed from state copies — this is a burned county. Jackson County was formed in 1819 from Cherokee Cession lands and is Alabama's northeast corner county. The Tennessee River valley attracted early settlers from Tennessee and Virginia. The Scottsboro Boys case (1931) is one of the most significant civil rights events in Alabama history. The Jackson County Historical Association has local materials. Russell Cave National Monument documents prehistoric habitation. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records. Paint Rock Valley and the Tennessee Valley Genealogical Society resources are valuable. ADAH holds county tax records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Jackson County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1819 from Blount County. County seat Birmingham (founded 1871). Alabama's most populous county. Birmingham was the iron and steel center of the South (Sloss Furnaces, TCI, US Steel). Major immigration destination for industrial workers. Extensive naturalization records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Birmingham. Jefferson County records are intact from 1819 and are the most voluminous in Alabama due to the county's size and Birmingham's industrial growth. Birmingham was founded in 1871 as an iron and steel city — Sloss Furnaces, TCI, and US Steel employment records document thousands of worker families. The Birmingham Public Library's Department of Archives and Manuscripts is a major genealogical repository with city directories, photograph collections, and manuscript collections. The Vulcan Park Archives document Birmingham's industrial heritage. For African American research, the Birmingham Civil Rights Institute has oral histories and community records. Jefferson County naturalization records are extensive due to heavy immigration of Italian, Greek, and Eastern European workers to the steel industry. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm coverage.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Jefferson County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1867 from Fayette, Marion, and Pickens as Jones County; renamed Sanford, then Lamar in 1877. County seat Vernon. Reconstruction-era formation. Strong Unionist sympathies in the area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Vernon. Created in 1867 from Fayette, Marion, and Pickens — check parent counties for earlier records. Originally named Jones County, then Sanford County, finally Lamar County in 1877. The county was formed during Reconstruction and had strong Unionist sympathies. The Fayette County courthouse fire (1866) affects some parent county records. Lamar County's economy was based on farming, timber, and later chicken farming. The Lamar County GenWeb project has volunteer-compiled genealogy data. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds and marriage records. Check ADAH for county tax records. The Vernon town square courthouse is on the National Register.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Lamar County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Cherokee and Chickasaw Cession — one of Alabama's original counties. County seat Florence. Florence planned by Cypress Land Company (1818). W.C. Handy birthplace. University of North Alabama. Tennessee Valley location.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Florence. Lauderdale County records are intact from 1818 — one of Alabama's original counties with excellent record survival. Florence was planned as a city by the Cypress Land Company (1818) and attracted prominent early settlers. W.C. Handy, 'Father of the Blues,' was born in Florence. Pope's Tavern Museum has local history materials. The Kennedy-Douglass Center for the Arts has archives. The University of North Alabama in Florence has manuscript collections. The Tennessee Valley Genealogical Society serves the area. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of deed books, probate records, and marriages. The Muscle Shoals area had Chickasaw and Cherokee connections — check removal records at NARA. ADAH holds county tax records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Lauderdale County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and bonds for Lauderdale County from 1818; Probate Judge issues marriage licenses in Alabama; FamilySearch has indexed many Alabama marriages

source_profiles.source_name: Lauderdale County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Lauderdale County; Alabama first enumerated in 1820 federal census; slave schedules in 1850 and 1860; agricultural and manufacturing schedules available

source_profiles.source_name: Lauderdale County Tax Records -- ADAH
source_profiles.repository: Alabama Department of Archives and History, Montgomery
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County tax assessment records for Lauderdale County listing property owners, acreage, livestock, and enslaved persons (pre-1865); ADAH holds microfilm of Alabama county tax records; essential for tracking family movements and wealth

source_profiles.source_name: Lauderdale County Federal Land Patents -- BLM General Land Office
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management / General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Lauderdale County from the General Land Office; Alabama is a public land state — original settlers purchased land from the federal government; patents searchable and downloadable at BLM GLO website

source_profiles.source_name: Lauderdale County Church and Cemetery Records
source_profiles.repository: ADAH / FamilySearch / Local churches and cemeteries
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Church records (Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian) for Lauderdale County; cemetery transcriptions at Find A Grave and USGenWeb; ADAH has some microfilmed church records; essential for vital events before state registration (1908)

county_metadata.record_losses: 1865 courthouse fire during Civil War destroyed many early records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Cherokee Cession — one of Alabama's original counties. County seat Moulton. 1865 courthouse fire during Civil War destroyed many early records. Courtland was an important antebellum cotton center. Jesse Owens birthplace (Oakville).
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Moulton. The 1865 courthouse fire during the Civil War destroyed many early records — this is a burned county. Check ADAH and FamilySearch for microfilm copies. Lawrence County was formed in 1818 from Cherokee Cession lands. Courtland, one of Alabama's oldest towns, was an important antebellum cotton center. The Joe Wheeler Plantation near Courtland has related family records. The Jesse Owens Memorial Park in Oakville honors the Olympic champion born in Lawrence County. The Bankhead National Forest covers part of the county. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records. The Lawrence County Historical Commission maintains local family files.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Lawrence County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books for Lawrence County from 1865; Probate Judge office in Moulton maintains originals; ADAH holds microfilm copies of historical deed books
source_profiles.survival_notes: Burned county: 1865 courthouse fire during Civil War destroyed many early records
